channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Opinion Poll: «The Household Case for Innovation: A Five-Country Survey on Smoking Cessation and Quality of Life»
item.description: "The Household Case for Innovation" survey by We Are Innovation interviews friends and family of ex-smokers in five countries, highlighting positive impacts of Innovative Nicotine Products (INPs) on cessation. Results show reduced secondhand smoke exposure and improved social interactions and health habits post-quitting, with strong support for INP access. Findings emphasize the influence of witnessing successful INP use on perceptions and advocate for including broader perspectives in tobacco control debates. Support for INP access correlates with personal experiences, challenging policymakers to consider a wider range of stakeholders. Experience-driven advocacy underscores the importance of involving affected communities in shaping tobacco control policies. (Published on May 25, 2026byweareinnovation.global)

item.title: GFN Insights #18 | SAVING LIVES AGAINST ALL ODDS | Harm Reduction in the Middle East and Africa
item.description: Harm reduction efforts in the Middle East and Africa face distinct challenges shaped by restrictive regulation, limited public awareness, and uneven access to safer nicotine alternatives. Advocates argue that in regions where smoking rates remain high, excluding tobacco harm reduction from public health strategies may slow progress in reducing smoking-related disease and mortality. The discussion highlights the importance of local evidence, consumer voices, and culturally relevant risk communication in shaping more effective nicotine policy. It also raises broader questions about whether global tobacco control frameworks adequately reflect the needs and realities of countries outside Europe and North America. (Published on May 26, 2026bygfn.tv)

item.title: "Third Hand" vaping in animal models
item.description: Research on third-hand exposure from vaping is adding new complexity to debates about nicotine product risk, toxicology, and public health communication. While animal models can provide useful insight into possible biological effects, researchers caution that findings must be interpreted carefully when assessing real-world human exposure. The debate highlights the importance of distinguishing between potential hazards observed under experimental conditions and actual population-level risk compared with cigarette smoke. It also reinforces the need for proportionate scientific communication that recognises uncertainty without obscuring the substantial risk differences between combustible and non-combustible nicotine products. (Published on May 26, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Panama’s Constitutional Ruling on Vaping: A Milestone for THR
item.description: Panama’s constitutional ruling on vaping adds to growing international debate over prohibition, consumer rights, and tobacco harm reduction policy. The decision raises questions about whether outright bans on lower-risk nicotine products are compatible with evidence-based public health objectives, particularly when combustible cigarettes remain legally available. Harm reduction advocates argue that regulated access to non-combustible alternatives may offer a more proportionate approach for adult smokers seeking to reduce their health risks. The case highlights wider tensions between restrictive nicotine regulation, constitutional principles, and strategies aimed at reducing smoking-related disease and mortality. (Published on May 26, 2026bytobaccoharmreduction.net)

item.title: Sweden hits smoke-free goal of under 5% daily smokers
item.description: Sweden has become the first European country to reach the smoke-free benchmark, with daily smoking falling below 5% of the population. The country’s long-term decline in cigarette use is increasingly being examined in relation to access to non-combustible nicotine products, including snus and nicotine pouches. Public health researchers continue to debate how Sweden’s experience should inform wider tobacco control strategies, particularly in countries where smoking prevalence remains significantly higher. The milestone reinforces the importance of distinguishing between nicotine use and the harms caused by tobacco combustion when designing evidence-based regulation. (Published on May 26, 2026bymedicalxpress.com)
